sections.content:

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Purple agent was a RESET button - clicking it restored all 5 greens. Board back to initial state. - Top: 3 greens at row 19, cols 24, 30, 36 - Bottom: 2 greens at col 42, rows 25, 37 - Need to find correct consumption sequence to solve level
- Plan: Select middle green (19,30) to see cross position, understand movement rules better.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 54 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so looking at the ascii:
Row 18: WWwNNwWWwNNwWWwwwwWWwNNwWWwwww
Row 19: WWNNNNWWNNNNWWwwwwWWNNNNWWwwww
Row 20: WWNNNNWWNNNNWWwwwwWWNNNNWWwwww
Row 21: WWwNNwWWwNNwWWwwwwWWwNNwWWwwww
The greens (N) are at:
- First green: cols 17-20 (center ~18-19)
- Second green: cols 23-26 (center ~24-25)  
- Third green: cols 35-38 (center ~36-37)
Wait, but the segmentation said centers at (19,18), (19,24), (19,36). So the greens are at columns 18, 24, and 36.
I clicked (19, 30) but there's no green at column 30. The click must have selected the green at column 24 (the middle one). Looking at the image, I see the middle green has a dark border, confirming it's selected.
But I don't see a cross! The segmentation doesn't show any gray cross object. This is strange - when I selected (19, 36) earlier, a cross appeared. Why doesn't it appear now?
Wait, maybe the cross only appears when there's a valid move. With 3 greens in a row, selecting the middle one might not produce a cross because there are greens on both sides. Let me check:
- When I selected (19, 36) (rightmost), cross appeared at (19, 24) - moving left toward the other greens
- When I selected (19, 30) which actually selected (19, 24) (middle), no cross appears
This suggests the cross only appears for edge greens (leftmost or rightmost), not for middle greens. The cross indicates the direction the green can move - toward the other greens.
So the mechanic is:
- Select an edge green (one with no neighbor on one side)
- Cross appears pointing toward the other greens
- Click cross to move the green, consuming any greens in the path
With 3 greens at cols 18, 24, 36:
- Selecting col 18 (leftmost): cross appears to the right, pointing toward col 24
- Selecting col 36 (rightmost): cross appears to the left, pointing toward col 24
- Selecting col 24 (middle): no cross (greens on both sides)
So the middle green can't move. Only edge greens can move.
Now, what's the goal? Let me think about what configurations could be the solution:
1. All greens consumed except some specific number
2. Greens aligned in a specific pattern
3. Greens moved to specific positions
Given that I can only move edge greens and they consume greens in their path, the possible moves are:
- Move col 18 right → consumes col 24, ends at some position (maybe col 36?)
- Move col 36 left → consumes col 24, ends at some position (maybe col 18?)
Wait, but earlier when I moved col 36 left, the cross appeared at col 24 (not col 18). And the green moved to col 24, consuming the green there. But there was also a green at col 18... Let me reconsider.
Actually, looking back at my earlier observations:
- When I had greens at (19, 30) and (19, 36), selecting (19, 36) produced a cross at (19, 24), and clicking it moved the green to (19, 24), consuming the green at (19, 30).
- Wait, that doesn't make sense. If the green moves from 36 to 24, it passes through 30 and consumes it. But 24 is to the left of 30, so the green would move past 30 to 24.
Hmm, but in the current state, the greens are at 18, 24, 36. If I select 36 and the cross appears at 18 (or 24?), clicking it would move the green from 36 to that position, consuming any greens in between.
Let me try selecting the leftmost green (19, 18) to see where the cross appears.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: 5 greens at (19,18), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42). Selected middle green (19,24) - no cross appears. Cross only appears for edge greens.
Plan: Select leftmost green (19,18) to see cross direction.
